title: Stack
description: The classic stack DSA problem: checking balanced parentheses, using LIFO order to match every closing bracket against its most recent opener.
objectives: Solve the balanced parentheses problem using a stack
Understand why LIFO order fits this problem naturally
Trace the algorithm step by step
Recognize the 'match against the most recent unmatched item' signal for stack

sections.text: The earlier stack topic covered push(), pop(), top(), and empty(). Here's the single most iconic problem that a stack solves cleanly.

sections.code:
// Is this string "balanced" — every opening bracket has a matching, correctly-ordered closer?
"(a+b)"      // balanced
"([a+b])"    // balanced
"(a+b]"      // NOT balanced — wrong closer
"((a+b)"     // NOT balanced — missing a closer

sections.text: 3. Why a Stack Fits This Problem

When a closing bracket appears, it must match the MOST RECENTLY opened, still-unmatched bracket — not just any earlier one. That's exactly LIFO (recall Stack Memory's plate analogy): the last opener pushed is the first one that needs to be matched and popped.

sections.title: Tracing "([a+b])"
points: End of string, stack is empty → balanced, return true

sections.title: Nested boxes, each needing to be closed in reverse order
sections.text: Recall the plate-stack analogy from Stack Memory. Opening nested boxes and closing them must happen in reverse order — the last box you opened is the first one you're able to close, because it's sitting right on top. A stack tracking open brackets works identically: the most recently opened one must be the next one matched and closed.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Checking for an empty stack too late — calling top() or pop() on an empty stack (e.g. an extra closing bracket with nothing open) is undefined; check empty() first.
Forgetting to check that the stack is empty at the very end — a string like "((a+b)" has every closer matched correctly, but leaves an unmatched opener still on the stack.
Matching a closer against the WRONG type of opener — top() must be checked for the correct matching bracket type, not just 'any' opener.
Using a queue instead of a stack — FIFO order would match brackets in the wrong order entirely; this problem specifically needs LIFO.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: Why is a stack the right data structure for checking balanced parentheses?
questions.answer: Because a closing bracket must match the most recently opened, still-unmatched opener — exactly LIFO order, which a stack naturally enforces.

questions.question: Walk through the algorithm for checking balanced parentheses using a stack.
questions.answer: Push every opening bracket onto the stack; for each closing bracket, check the stack isn't empty and its top matches the correct opener type, then pop it. At the end, the string is balanced only if the stack is empty.

questions.question: What are the two ways a string can fail the balanced-parentheses check?
questions.answer: A closing bracket appears with no matching opener available (empty stack, or wrong opener type on top), or the string ends with unmatched openers still left on the stack.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Balanced parentheses is the classic stack problem: push every opening bracket, and for every closing bracket, check that the stack isn't empty and its top element is the matching opener type before popping it. The string is balanced only if every closer found a correct match and the stack ends empty, meaning no unmatched openers remain. This works because a closer must always match the most recently opened, still-unmatched bracket — exactly the LIFO order a stack enforces, the same principle behind the function call stack from Stack Memory.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Stack (DSA)
sections.text: Balanced parentheses = nested boxes, close the most recently opened one first. Push openers, pop-and-check on closers, balanced only if the stack ends empty.

sections.title: Valid Parentheses
sections.problemStatement: Given a string s containing just '(', ')', '{', '}', '[', ']', determine if the input string is valid (every bracket properly closed in the correct order).
hints: This is exactly this lesson's own balanced-parentheses example — no punctuation-and-letters mix, just brackets.
Push every opening bracket. For every closing bracket, the stack must be non-empty and its top must be the matching opener.
Balanced only if every closer matched correctly AND the stack is empty at the end (no leftover unmatched openers).

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Repeatedly find and remove any adjacent matching pair (like "()", "[]", "{}") from the string until no more removals are possible.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: This lesson's own stack-based approach: push openers, pop-and-check on closers.
